text: Maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max) is primarily limited by the ability of the cardiorespiratory system to deliver oxygen to exercising muscles, not by skeletal muscle oxygen extraction capacity.
The increase in VO2max with endurance training results primarily from an increase in maximal cardiac output rather than an increase in arteriovenous oxygen difference.
Manipulations that alter oxygen delivery (blood doping, hypoxia, or beta-blockade) produce corresponding changes in VO2max, confirming oxygen delivery as the primary limiter.

Endurance training increases mitochondrial enzyme activities in skeletal muscle, which improves submaximal performance by enhancing fat oxidation and decreasing lactic acid accumulation at a given VO2.
VO2max sets the upper limit for endurance performance because an athlete cannot sustain exercise above 100% VO2max for extended periods.
Distance running performance is determined by three key physiological variables: VO2max, running economy, and fractional utilization of VO2max.
Speed at lactate threshold is the best single physiological predictor of distance running performance because it integrates VO2max, running economy, and fractional utilization of VO2max.
